questions.question: Principle: A person is guilty of theft if he dishonestly takes movable property without consent. Facts: Rohan picks up a pen thinking it is his, but it belongs to the shop. Is Rohan guilty of theft?
questions.explanation: Yahan sabse important hai dishonest intention. Rohan ko laga pen uska hai → matlab usne chori karne ka socha hi nahi. Intention nahi = theft nahi. Isliye guilty nahi.

questions.question: Principle: A contract without free consent is voidable. Facts: A forces B to sign a contract at gunpoint. What is the nature of this contract?
questions.explanation: Gunpoint = force = free consent nahi. B chahe to contract cancel kar sakta hai. Isliye voidable (optional cancel).

questions.question: Principle: Ignorance of law is not an excuse. Facts: Aman breaks a law saying he didn’t know it existed. Is Aman liable?
questions.explanation: Law bolta hai: “Mujhe nahi pata tha” = excuse nahi. Sabko law pata hona chahiye maana jata hai. Aman phir bhi punish hoga.

questions.question: Principle: A person has the right of private defense. Facts: X hits Y to protect himself from Y’s attack. Is X guilty?
questions.explanation: Self-defense allowed hai. Agar koi attack kare, tu apne aap ko bacha sakta hai. X ne defence mein maara = no crime.

questions.question: Principle: A minor cannot enter into a valid contract. Facts: A 16-year-old signs a loan agreement. What is the status of this agreement?
questions.explanation: Minor (18 se chhota) legal contract nahi kar sakta. Jo bhi karega wo start se hi invalid hai. Isliye void.

questions.question: Principle: Defamation means harming someone’s reputation. Facts: A publishes false statements about B in a newspaper. What offence has A committed?
questions.explanation: False news → reputation damage. Reputation ko harm karna = defamation. Clear case.

questions.question: Principle: Consent obtained by fraud is not valid. Facts: X lies and sells a product to Y. Is Y's consent valid?
questions.explanation: Jhoot bolke consent li → fraud. Fraud wali consent valid nahi hoti. Y ka consent invalid.

questions.question: Principle: Agreement without consideration is void. Facts: A promises to give B ₹10,000 as a gift. What is the status of this agreement?
questions.explanation: Contract = give & take hona chahiye. Yahan kuch return nahi mil raha. Isliye agreement valid nahi.

questions.question: Principle: Negligence is failure to take reasonable care. Facts: A drives rashly and injures a pedestrian. Is A liable?
questions.explanation: Driver ko dhyan se chalana chahiye tha. Rash driving = care nahi li. Injury hui → negligence.

questions.question: Principle: Assault is creating fear of harm. Facts: X raises a stick to hit Y but does not hit. What offence has X committed?
questions.explanation: Maarna zaroori nahi hai. Sirf darr create karna bhi assault hai. Stick uthaya → Y dar gaya → assault.

questions.question: Principle: A person is liable for negligence if he fails to act as a reasonable person. Facts: A leaves a pit open on the road without warning signs. B falls into it and gets injured. Is A liable?
questions.explanation: A ko reasonable care lena chahiye tha (warning sign lagana). Usne nahi lagaya → dusre ko injury hui → negligence ban gaya.

questions.question: Principle: Consent is valid only when given freely. Facts: X threatens Y to sell his house, and Y agrees out of fear. What is the nature of this contract?
questions.explanation: Threat = free consent nahi hai. Y chahe to contract cancel kar sakta hai, isliye voidable.

questions.question: Principle: A person is guilty of battery if he intentionally uses force without consent. Facts: A slaps B during an argument. What offence has A committed?
questions.explanation: Yahan actual physical contact hua (slap). Assault fear hota hai, battery mein touch/force hota hai. Isliye battery.

questions.question: Principle: A person is not liable for an act done by accident without negligence. Facts: A is driving carefully, but suddenly a child runs in front and gets injured. Is A liable?
questions.explanation: A careful driving kar raha tha. Accident unavoidable tha. Negligence nahi hai, to liability nahi banti.

questions.question: Principle: A person who instigates another to commit a crime is also liable. Facts: X encourages Y to steal a bike. Y steals it. Who is guilty?
options: Both X and Y are guilty
questions.correctAnswer: Both X and Y are guilty
questions.explanation: Y ne theft kiya, aur X ne usko uksaya (instigate kiya). Dono liable hote hain.

questions.question: Principle: A contract made under mistake of fact is void. Facts: A agrees to buy a horse from B, but the horse was already dead. What is the status of this contract?
questions.explanation: Dono ko laga horse zinda hai, but wo pehle hi mar chuka tha. Ye mutual mistake hai → contract void.

questions.question: Principle: Defamation requires a false statement communicated to others. Facts: A tells B privately that C is a thief, but no one else hears it. Is this defamation?
questions.explanation: Defamation tab hota hai jab third person tak baat pahunchti hai. Yahan sirf B ko bola, public tak nahi gaya, to defamation nahi.

questions.question: Principle: Trespass means entering someone’s property without permission. Facts: A enters B’s land without permission to take a shortcut. What offence has A committed?
questions.explanation: Permission ke bina kisi ki property mein ghusna = trespass. Chahe intention shortcut ka ho, phir bhi offence hai.

questions.question: Principle: A person is liable for misrepresentation if he makes a false statement believing it to be true. Facts: A sells a car to B saying it is new, but A genuinely believed it was new. What is A liable for?
questions.explanation: A ne jhoot bola, but usko laga wo sach hai. Ye fraud nahi, misrepresentation hai.

questions.question: Principle: A person cannot transfer a better title than he himself has. Facts: A steals a watch and sells it to B. B buys it in good faith. Who remains the owner of the watch?
questions.explanation: A chor hai, uske paas valid ownership hi nahi thi. Wo B ko ownership transfer nahi kar sakta. Isliye original owner hi owner rahega.

questions.question: Who continued as the Prime Minister of India after forming the government in 2024 and is serving in 2025–26?
questions.explanation: Narendra Modi continued as the Prime Minister of India after forming the government in 2024 and is serving in 2025-26.

questions.explanation: Sanjiv Khanna is serving as the Chief Justice of India in 2025–26 according to the text.

questions.explanation: India focused on the India Semiconductor Mission to boost semiconductor manufacturing in 2025–26.

questions.explanation: Uttarakhand implemented the Uniform Civil Code (UCC) in 2025.

questions.explanation: Newcastle Disease is a highly contagious disease of birds caused by a virus.

questions.explanation: Prevention of Blindness Week is observed annually in India from April 1 to April 7.

df8e9557-df5c-4c52-a2da-4b2911e4b96c.filename: LEGAL APTITUDE.pdf + PU LAW (Day-2).pdf + PU LAW Current Affairs (Day-1).pdf

questions.question: Principle: A person is guilty of theft if he dishonestly takes movable property without consent. Facts: Rohan picks up a pen thinking it is his, but it belongs to the shop.
questions.explanation: Dishonest intention is important for theft. Rohan thought the pen was his, so he had no intention to steal, therefore not guilty.

questions.question: Principle: A contract without free consent is voidable. Facts: A forces B to sign a contract at gunpoint.
questions.explanation: Consent obtained at gunpoint is not free consent, making the contract voidable at B's option.

questions.question: Principle: Ignorance of law is not an excuse. Facts: Aman breaks a law saying he didn’t know it existed.
questions.explanation: The principle states that not knowing a law is not a valid excuse, so Aman is liable.

questions.question: Principle: A person has the right of private defense. Facts: X hits Y to protect himself from Y’s attack.
questions.explanation: Self-defense is allowed. X acted to protect himself, thus not committing a crime.

questions.question: Principle: A minor cannot enter into a valid contract. Facts: A 16-year-old signs a loan agreement.
questions.explanation: A minor (under 18) cannot enter into a legal contract, making any such agreement invalid from the start.

questions.question: Principle: Defamation means harming someone’s reputation. Facts: A publishes false statements about B in a newspaper.
questions.explanation: Publishing false statements that damage reputation is a clear case of defamation.

questions.question: Principle: Consent obtained by fraud is not valid. Facts: X lies and sells a product to Y.
questions.explanation: Consent obtained through lies constitutes fraud, making the consent invalid.

questions.question: Principle: Agreement without consideration is void. Facts: A promises to give B ₹10,000 as a gift.
questions.explanation: For a contract to be valid, there must be a 'give and take' (consideration). A gift lacks consideration, making the agreement void.

questions.question: Principle: Negligence is failure to take reasonable care. Facts: A drives rashly and injures a pedestrian.
questions.explanation: Rash driving demonstrates a failure to take reasonable care, leading to liability for negligence when injury occurs.

questions.question: Principle: Assault is creating fear of harm. Facts: X raises a stick to hit Y but does not hit.
questions.explanation: Assault involves creating a fear of harm, even if no physical contact is made. Raising a stick causes fear.

questions.question: Principle: A person is liable for negligence if he fails to act as a reasonable person. Facts: A leaves a pit open on the road without warning signs. B falls into it and gets injured.
questions.explanation: A failed to take reasonable care by not putting up a warning sign, leading to B's injury, hence A is liable for negligence.

questions.question: Principle: Consent is valid only when given freely. Facts: X threatens Y to sell his house, and Y agrees out of fear.
questions.explanation: Consent given under threat is not free, making the contract voidable at Y's discretion.

questions.question: Principle: A person is guilty of battery if he intentionally uses force without consent. Facts: A slaps B during an argument.
questions.explanation: Battery involves actual physical contact or use of force without consent, which a slap constitutes.

questions.question: Principle: A person is not liable for an act done by accident without negligence. Facts: A is driving carefully, but suddenly a child runs in front and gets injured.
questions.explanation: Since A was driving carefully and the accident was unavoidable, there was no negligence, and thus no liability.

questions.question: Principle: A person who instigates another to commit a crime is also liable. Facts: X encourages Y to steal a bike. Y steals it.
options: Both X and Y are guilty
questions.correctAnswer: Both X and Y are guilty
questions.explanation: X instigated Y to commit theft, making both X and Y liable under the given principle.

questions.question: Principle: A contract made under mistake of fact is void. Facts: A agrees to buy a horse from B, but the horse was already dead.
questions.explanation: This is a mutual mistake of fact regarding the existence of the subject matter, rendering the contract void.

questions.question: Principle: Defamation requires a false statement communicated to others. Facts: A tells B privately that C is a thief, but no one else hears it.
questions.explanation: Defamation requires communication to a third person. Since only B heard it, it's not defamation.

questions.question: Principle: Trespass means entering someone’s property without permission. Facts: A enters B’s land without permission to take a shortcut.
questions.explanation: Entering someone's property without permission, regardless of intention (like taking a shortcut), constitutes trespass.

questions.question: Principle: A person is liable for misrepresentation if he makes a false statement believing it to be true. Facts: A sells a car to B saying it is new, but A genuinely believed it was new.
questions.explanation: Since A genuinely believed the false statement to be true, it is misrepresentation, not fraud (which requires dishonest intent).

questions.question: Principle: A person cannot transfer a better title than he himself has. Facts: A steals a watch and sells it to B. B buys it in good faith.
questions.explanation: A thief has no valid title to the property and therefore cannot transfer ownership to anyone, even a good-faith buyer. The original owner retains title.
